title: Budget-Aware Adaptive Adversarial Patches for Black-Box Object Detection
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP 2026)
summary: Adversarial patches pose a practical threat to modern object detectors. Prior work shows vulnerability, but three gaps limit actionable insight: (i) few \emph{score-based black-box} attacks \emph{jointly} optimize patch \emph{location, texture, and size} under tight query budgets; (ii) success is rarely tied to the patch's \emph{visual footprint}; and (iii) evaluations often conflate EOT robustness with plain-view suppression. We present \method{}, a query-efficient, budget-adaptive black-box attack that couples a lightweight \emph{Contextual Thompson-Sampling} placer with NES-style pixel updates, growing the patch only when progress stalls. Reporting is anchored by a \emph{strict plain-image} suppression test; EOT is audited but never used as a substitute for success, and optional appearance/printability weights expose strength--visibility trade-offs. Across YOLOv5, Faster R-CNN, and YOLOS, \method{} achieves strong suppression on CNN-based detectors and substantial suppression on the transformer-based detector, using compact patches and exposing clear query--footprint trade-offs relative to fixed-size and heuristic baselines. A print--capture pilot further shows transfer across unseen physical objects and viewpoints.

AI.tldr: A query-efficient black-box attack (Contextual Thompson-Sampling + NES) that jointly optimizes adversarial patch location, texture, and size, with strict plain-image evaluation revealing clear query-footprint trade-offs across CNN and transformer detectors.
AI.motivation: Prior work on adversarial patches has three gaps: (i) few score-based black-box attacks jointly optimize patch location, texture, and size under tight query budgets; (ii) success is rarely tied to the patch's visual footprint; (iii) evaluations conflate EOT robustness with plain-view suppression.
AI.method: A budget-adaptive black-box attack coupling Contextual Thompson-Sampling (for patch placement) with NES-style pixel updates, growing the patch only when progress stalls. Uses strict plain-image suppression tests, with optional appearance/printability weights to expose strength-visibility trade-offs.
AI.result: Strong suppression on CNN-based detectors (YOLOv5, Faster R-CNN) and substantial suppression on transformer-based detector (YOLOS) using compact patches, with clear query-footprint trade-offs versus baselines. Print-capture pilot shows transfer across unseen physical objects and viewpoints.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively uncovers strength-visibility trade-offs in adversarial patch attacks, achieving strong suppression with compact patches while revealing previously overlooked query-footprint dynamics.

title: CAOA -- Completion-Assisted Object-CAD Alignment
summary: Accurately aligning CAD models to their corresponding objects in indoor RGB-D scans is a central challenge in 3D semantic reconstruction. The task requires estimating a 9-Degree-of-Freedom (DoF) pose-position, rotation, and scale along three axes-but is hindered by noisy and incomplete scans, as well as segmentation errors that cause geometric distortions. We present Completion-Assisted Object-CAD Alignment (CAOA), a method that integrates a semantically and contextually aware point cloud completion module with a symmetry-aware relative pose estimation algorithm, enabling precise alignment of CAD models to scanned objects. Existing completion methods are typically trained and evaluated on synthetic datasets, which often fail to generalize to real-world scans. To bridge this gap, we introduce a synthetic data generation strategy tailored to indoor scenes, significantly reducing the synthetic-to-real domain gap-validated through quantitative comparisons with widely used completion datasets. In addition, we release S2C-Completion, an expert-annotated dataset of over 8,500 object-CAD pairs from Scan2CAD, created for real-world indoor single-object completion and intended as a new benchmark for this task. For object-CAD alignment, we incorporate symmetry information via a symmetry-aware loss, improving robustness to symmetric ambiguities. On the Scan2CAD benchmark, CAOA achieves a 17% accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: CAOA integrates point cloud completion with symmetry-aware pose estimation to accurately align CAD models to objects in indoor RGB-D scans, achieving 17% accuracy improvement on Scan2CAD benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle with noisy, incomplete scans and segmentation errors. Also, completion models trained on synthetic data generalize poorly to real-world scans.
AI.method: CAOA uses a semantically and contextually aware point cloud completion module plus a symmetry-aware relative pose estimation algorithm. It also introduces a synthetic data generation strategy and releases the S2C-Completion dataset with over 8,500 expert-annotated object-CAD pairs.
AI.result: On Scan2CAD benchmark, CAOA achieves a 17% accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Symmetry-aware loss improves robustness to symmetric ambiguities, and the synthetic data generation strategy effectively reduces the synthetic-to-real domain gap.

title: RegimeVGGT: Layer-Wise Spatially Preserving Redundancy Removal for Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 7 tables. Jinhao You, Shuo Lyu, Zhuohang Lyu, Tanxuan Li, and Zibo Zhao contributed equally. Shuo Lyu is the corresponding author
summary: Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT) recovers dense 3D scene structure from multi-view images in one forward pass, but quadratic cross-frame attention limits its scalability. Existing training-free accelerators reduce computation uniformly along one axis, missing layer heterogeneity. Our spectral, probing, and causal analyses reveal three regimes: shallow layers lack cross-view structure, middle layers drive cross-view alignment, and deep layers are redundant for dense geometry yet their cross-frame attention remains essential for pose. RegimeVGGT applies layer-wise U-shaped compression along two axes: Saliency-Guided Banded Merging protects geometry- and edge-salient tokens, while Selectively Protected K/V Downsampling preserves cross-frame spatial coverage and the pose-critical path through a phase-shifted spatial grid, a reference-frame anchor, and uncompressed camera/register tokens. Training-free, RegimeVGGT achieves a 6.7x speedup over VGGT* at matched reconstruction quality.

AI.tldr: RegimeVGGT accelerates the VGGT model by 6.7x through layer-wise U-shaped compression strategies that exploit three distinct processing regimes, without requiring additional training.
AI.motivation: Existing VGGT has quadratic cross-frame attention that limits scalability, and previous accelerators apply uniform reduction missing layer heterogeneity.
AI.method: Proposes RegimeVGGT with layer-wise U-shaped compression along two axes: (1) Saliency-Guided Banded Merging protecting geometry/edge-salient tokens, and (2) Selectively Protected K/V Downsampling preserving spatial coverage and pose-critical paths via phase-shifted grid, reference-frame anchor, and uncompressed camera/register tokens.
AI.result: Achieves 6.7x speedup over VGGT* at matched reconstruction quality in a training-free manner.
AI.conclusion: By identifying three regimes (shallow lacking cross-view structure, middle driving alignment, deep redundant for geometry but pose-essential), layer-wise heterogeneous compression effectively accelerates dense 3D reconstruction.

title: Reasoning as Intersection: Consensus-Frame Alignment for Visual Focus in Video-MLLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning has improved the reasoning ability of large language models, but applying outcome-only rewards to video multimodal large language models (Video-MLLMs) provides limited guidance on which visual evidence should support the answer. Inspired by multisensory integration, where consistent cues can enhance the salience and reliability of perceptual estimates, we introduce Consensus Frame GRPO (CF-GRPO), a temporal-annotation-free process-level reward framework for evidence-aware video reasoning. CF-GRPO constructs a consensus frame prior from intrinsic video cues, including temporal coverage, scene-transition cues, and query-conditioned visual relevance. It then computes a model-side frame-use score from visual and response representations and optimizes their agreement through the Consensus Frame Reward (CFR). With salience-aware sparse aggregation and distribution sharpening, CFR provides a high-contrast reward signal without requiring human temporal annotations. Experiments show that VideoCFR achieves competitive performance across complex video reasoning benchmarks and improves several metrics over representative Video-MLLM and RL baselines, while the consensus prior provides an interpretable view of the evidence frames emphasized during training. The implementation is available at https://github.com/1Pansy/VideoCFR.

AI.tldr: CF-GRPO is a process-level reward framework for video reasoning that uses consensus frame priors from intrinsic video cues to provide evidence-aware rewards without temporal annotations, improving Video-MLLM reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Outcome-only rewards for Video-MLLMs provide limited guidance on which visual evidence supports the answer, lacking temporal annotation and process-level feedback.
AI.method: CF-GRPO constructs a consensus frame prior from temporal coverage, scene-transition cues, and query-conditioned visual relevance; computes a model-side frame-use score; and optimizes agreement via Consensus Frame Reward with salience-aware sparse aggregation and distribution sharpening.
AI.result: VideoCFR achieves competitive performance on complex video reasoning benchmarks and improves several metrics over representative Video-MLLM and RL baselines.
AI.conclusion: The consensus prior provides interpretable evidence frames, and CF-GRPO enhances video reasoning without requiring human temporal annotations.

title: Domain Generalizable Adaptation of 3D Vision-Language Models via Regularized Fine-Tuning
comment: Accepted at Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Domain adaptation remains a central challenge in 3D vision, especially for multimodal foundation models that align 3D point clouds with visual and textual data. While these models demonstrate strong general capabilities, adapting them to downstream domains with limited data often leads to overfitting and catastrophic forgetting. To address this, we introduce ReFine3D, a regularized fine-tuning framework designed for domain-generalizable tuning of 3D large multimodal models (LMMs). ReFine3D combines selective layer tuning with two targeted regularization strategies: multi-view consistency across augmented point clouds and text diversity through synonym-based prompts generated by large language models. Additionally, we incorporate point-rendered vision supervision and a test-time augmentation mechanism with confidence-based aggregation to further enhance robustness. Extensive experiments across different 3D domain generalization benchmarks show that ReFine3D improves base-to-novel class generalization by 1.36%, cross-dataset transfer by 2.43%, robustness to corruption by 1.80%, and few-shot accuracy by up to 3.11%, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods with minimal added computational overhead.

AI.tldr: ReFine3D is a regularized fine-tuning framework for 3D large multimodal models that prevents overfitting and catastrophic forgetting during domain adaptation, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks with minimal computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Domain adaptation is a central challenge in 3D vision; multimodal foundation models aligning 3D point clouds with visual/textual data suffer from overfitting and catastrophic forgetting when adapted to downstream domains with limited data.
AI.method: ReFine3D combines selective layer tuning with two regularization strategies: (1) multi-view consistency across augmented point clouds, and (2) text diversity via synonym-based prompts from LLMs. It also incorporates point-rendered vision supervision and test-time augmentation with confidence-based aggregation.
AI.result: Improves base-to-novel class generalization by 1.36%, cross-dataset transfer by 2.43%, robustness to corruption by 1.80%, and few-shot accuracy by up to 3.11%, outperforming prior SOTA methods with minimal added computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: ReFine3D effectively enables domain-generalizable tuning for 3D LMMs, addressing key limitations in domain adaptation while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Data-Forcing Distillation: Restoring Diversity and Fidelity in Few-Step Video Generation
summary: Recent progress has shown promise in distilling multi-step video diffusion models into efficient few-step students. Among them, Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) and its successor DMD2 achieved strong generation quality and fast convergence. However, due to the nature of the reverse Kullback--Leibler (KL) objective, these methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: a substantial drop in sample diversity, and visibly over-saturated outputs that deviate from real-video appearance. In this work, we propose Data-Forcing Distillation (DFD), a simple post-training framework that restores diversity and fidelity in DMD with only a single-line of code change. At its core is the teacher score discrepancy to guide the student toward the real-data distribution, pulling it to missing modes (mitigating mode collapse) and away from problematic modes absent in real data (avoiding over-saturation). We provide an in-depth theoretical analysis of our framework and validate our approach on text-to-video, image-to-video, and autoregressive video generation. With only 100--300 steps of finetuning, DFD effectively restores diversity and fidelity on both Wan2.1-1.3B and Cosmos-Predict2.5-2B model, resolving the over-saturation artifacts with significantly better video dynamics and appearance, and even outperforms the teacher model.

AI.tldr: Proposes Data-Forcing Distillation (DFD), a simple post-training framework that fixes mode collapse and over-saturation issues in DMD-based video diffusion distillation with just one line of code change, restoring diversity and fidelity.
AI.motivation: Existing DMD/DMD2 methods suffer from two failure modes: (1) significant drop in sample diversity, and (2) over-saturated outputs that deviate from real-video appearance, both due to the reverse KL objective.
AI.method: Introduces teacher score discrepancy to guide the student toward real-data distribution - pulling it toward missing modes (mitigating mode collapse) and away from problematic modes absent in real data (avoiding over-saturation). Requires only a single line of code change.
AI.result: With only 100-300 finetuning steps, DFD restores diversity and fidelity on Wan2.1-1.3B and Cosmos-Predict2.5-2B models, resolves over-saturation artifacts with better video dynamics and appearance, and even outperforms the teacher model.
AI.conclusion: A simple yet effective post-training framework validated across text-to-video, image-to-video, and autoregressive video generation tasks.

title: Vines-DB: An RGB image dataset for multi-species ornamental vine segmentation
comment: 7 pages, 1 figure. Source data repository: OSF (DOI: 10.17605/OSF.IO/YJHCK)
summary: The Vines-DB dataset contains 1,218 original high-resolution RGB images of seven ornamental vine species collected under field conditions at the Utah Agricultural Experiment Station's Greenville Research Farm in Logan, Utah, USA. The dataset was generated from 168 individual vine plants that were transplanted in 2022 and photographed repeatedly across multiple months during the 2023 and 2024 growing seasons (July-October). Images were captured with an iPhone 16 Pro equipped with a 48 MP camera between 10:00 AM and 12:00 PM under daylight. Vines were grown on 1.2m x 2.4m trellises and photographed from a distance of 1m against black or white Styrofoam backdrops to improve contrast and reduce background noise. The dataset includes Akebia quinata, Campsis radicans, Hydrangea anomala petiolaris, Lonicera x heckrottii, Campsis x tagliabuana 'Madame Galen', Parthenocissus quinquefolia, and Wisteria floribunda. All original images were manually annotated in Roboflow by trained annotators to produce polygon-based instance segmentation masks for eight classes, including seven species and background. After preprocessing and data augmentation, the working dataset was expanded to 2,307 images for model development and evaluation. The augmented dataset was divided into 2,019 training images, 192 validation images, and 96 test images using stratified sampling to maintain balanced representation. Vines-DB supports the development and evaluation of deep learning models for multi-class instance segmentation in precision horticulture and urban ecology. The dataset enables applications such as automated canopy cover estimation, species identification, and scalable field phenotyping. In addition, repeated monthly imaging of the plants captures temporal variation in canopy development and plant appearance, increasing the dataset's utility for segmentation benchmarking under realistic field conditions.

AI.tldr: Vines-DB is a dataset of 1,218 high-resolution RGB images of seven ornamental vine species, annotated for instance segmentation and augmented to 2,307 images, collected over two growing seasons under field conditions to support deep learning in precision horticulture.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of publicly available, field-collected vine datasets for developing deep learning models for multi-class instance segmentation in precision horticulture and urban ecology.
AI.method: Images of 168 vine plants (7 species) were captured with an iPhone 16 Pro (48 MP) under controlled field conditions using black/white backdrops. Polygon-based instance segmentation masks were manually annotated via Roboflow. Data augmentation expanded the dataset to 2,307 images, split into training (2,019), validation (192), and test (96) sets using stratified sampling.
AI.result: A curated, augmented dataset of 2,307 images with balanced class representation, covering temporal variation across months and years, ready for multi-class instance segmentation model development.
AI.conclusion: Vines-DB enables automated canopy cover estimation, species identification, and scalable field phenotyping, serving as a benchmark for segmentation under realistic field conditions.

title: Neural Phase Correlation
summary:
Correspondence is fundamentally relational: it seeks the unknown transformation between two observations of a common scene, not the content of either. Yet the dominant learning-based methods do not represent the transformation as a first-class object in the architecture. They encode each image independently and let a learned similarity function or a deep decoder discover the mapping implicitly. Phase correlation is the canonical exception, measuring the inter-image relationship directly in the Fourier domain, but the rigidity of its fixed basis confines it to global translation.
  We introduce a learned generalization of phase correlation that lifts this restriction by learning the basis on which the transformation decomposes. The same algebraic primitive extends to dense non-rigid deformations and to unitary dynamics. On the ACDC cardiac-MRI benchmark the framework matches or exceeds prior published baselines on both registration directions. On CAMUS echocardiography it matches state-of-the-art without auxiliary scoring or adaptive-smoothness mechanisms. Applied to time-evolved wavefunction pairs of the 1-D quantum harmonic oscillator, the same framework recovers the Hermite-function eigenstates and the quantized energy levels of the unknown Hamiltonian from observation pairs alone.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a learned generalization of phase correlation that treats transformation as a first-class object, learning basis functions for image correspondence. It applies to dense non-rigid deformations and unitary dynamics, matching/exceeding baselines on cardiac MRI/echocardiography benchmarks and recovering quantum eigenstates from observation pairs.
AI.motivation: Current learning-based correspondence methods encode images independently and discover transformations implicitly, rather than treating the transformation itself as a first-class object. Phase correlation directly measures inter-image relationships but is limited to global translations due to its fixed Fourier basis.
AI.method: A learned generalization of phase correlation that learns the basis on which the transformation decomposes, lifting the rigidity restriction of fixed Fourier bases. The framework extends to dense non-rigid deformations and unitary dynamics.
AI.result: Matches/exceeds prior published baselines on ACDC cardiac-MRI benchmark (both registration directions). Matches state-of-the-art on CAMUS echocardiography without auxiliary scoring or adaptive-smoothness mechanisms. Recovers Hermite-function eigenstates and quantized energy levels of the unknown Hamiltonian from observation pairs of 1-D quantum harmonic oscillator.
AI.conclusion: Treating transformation as a first-class object via learned phase correlation is a powerful and general framework applicable across medical imaging and quantum physics, achieving strong results without task-specific mechanisms.

title: Architectural Bias in Face Presentation Attack Detection: A Comparative Study of Vision Transformers and Convolutional Neural Networks
summary: Face Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) systems constitute a critical security layer in biometric authentication; however, existing approaches exhibit systematic performance disparities across demographic groups, disproportionately affecting individuals with darker skin tones. This paper presents a comparative empirical investigation of whether Vision Transformer architectures reduce demographic bias in face PAD systems relative to convolutional baselines. Experiments are conducted on the CASIA-SURF Cross-Ethnicity Face Anti-Spoofing (CeFA) dataset. Three architectures are evaluated: a Multimodal ViT-Tiny trained from scratch, a ResNet18 CNN baseline, and a pretrained DeiT-S fine-tuned on CeFA across African, East Asian, and zero-shot Central Asian demographic groups. DeiT-S achieves the highest overall accuracy of 97.27% and the lowest EER of 0.86%, outperforming ResNet18 at 90.15% accuracy. In terms of fairness, DeiT-S reduces the inter-ethnic ACER gap between African and East Asian subjects to 0.13%, compared to 0.75% reported in an LBP-based work [6], representing an 83% reduction. Most notably, while ResNet18 records a BPCER of 10.44% on zero-shot Central Asian subjects, DeiT-S maintains 2.89% on the same unseen group, demonstrating a 3.6x generalization advantage. These results suggest that pretrained Vision Transformers achieve superior PAD accuracy, produce smaller demographic performance gaps, and generalize more equitably across unseen demographic groups, indicating that cross-demographic fairness in PAD may partly be influenced by architectural design.

AI.tldr: This paper compares Vision Transformers (ViTs) against CNNs for face anti-spoofing and finds that pretrained ViTs (DeiT-S) achieve higher accuracy (97.27%), lower demographic bias (0.13% ACER gap between ethnic groups), and better cross-demographic generalization (3.6x improvement on unseen groups) compared to ResNet18, suggesting architectural choices influence fairness in PAD.
AI.motivation: Existing face PAD systems show systematic performance disparities across demographic groups, disproportionately affecting individuals with darker skin tones. The authors investigate whether Vision Transformer architectures can reduce this demographic bias compared to convolutional baselines.
AI.method: Comparative empirical investigation on the CASIA-SURF CeFA dataset, evaluating three architectures: Multimodal ViT-Tiny (trained from scratch), ResNet18 CNN (baseline), and pretrained DeiT-S (fine-tuned) across African, East Asian, and zero-shot Central Asian demographic groups.
AI.result: DeiT-S achieves 97.27% accuracy and 0.86% EER (vs. ResNet18's 90.15%). DeiT-S reduces inter-ethnic ACER gap to 0.13% (83% reduction vs. LBP-based work's 0.75%). For zero-shot Central Asian subjects, DeiT-S maintains 2.89% BPCER vs. ResNet18's 10.44% — a 3.6x generalization advantage.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained Vision Transformers achieve superior PAD accuracy, produce smaller demographic performance gaps, and generalize more equitably across unseen demographic groups, suggesting cross-demographic fairness in PAD may be partly influenced by architectural design choices.

title: A Prototypical Signature Approach for Writer-Independent Offline Signature Verification
comment: Accepted for oral presentation at the International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) 2026
summary: Offline handwritten signature verification aims to distinguish genuine from forged signatures using static images. Since real forgeries are rarely available, negative samples are usually randomly drawn from genuine signatures of other users to create training data. However, this random selection often lacks diversity, increases redundancy, and escalates computational cost, leading to inefficient training. We propose a data-driven strategy to generate diverse, informative negative samples using prototypical signatures, which are compact, non-identifiable summaries of genuine signature features. Based on the experiments results, we conclude that (i) prototypical signatures yield more informative negative samples, improving the detection of skilled forgeries; (ii) the proposed approach is backbone-agnostic, showing robustness across architectures; and (iii) when combined with a primal-form linear SVM, it serves as an alternative to RBF-based models while significantly improving scalability and computational efficiency. Implementation of the method is available at https://github.com/kdmoura/proto_hsv.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a data-driven strategy using prototypical signatures (compact, non-identifiable summaries) to generate diverse, informative negative samples for offline handwritten signature verification, improving skilled forgery detection, showing backbone-agnostic robustness, and offering efficient scalability when combined with linear SVM.
AI.motivation: Real forgeries are rarely available, and random negative sampling from other users' genuine signatures lacks diversity, increases redundancy, and escalates computational cost, leading to inefficient training.
AI.method: A data-driven strategy that uses prototypical signatures—compact, non-identifiable summaries of genuine signature features—to generate diverse and informative negative samples for training.
AI.result: (i) Prototypical signatures yield more informative negative samples, improving skilled forgery detection; (ii) the approach is backbone-agnostic, robust across architectures; (iii) combined with primal-form linear SVM, it serves as an alternative to RBF-based models with improved scalability and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed prototypical signature-based negative sampling strategy effectively addresses the forgery scarcity problem, enhances verification performance, and improves computational scalability.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Modal Retrieval for Knowledge-Grounded News Image Captioning
summary: Traditional image captioning methods often struggle to generate comprehensive, context-rich descriptions, especially for details not directly observable from visual cues. To overcome this, we propose a novel retrieval-augmented image captioning framework that generates captions with deeper insights, such as object attributes, event context, and underlying significance, by leveraging external knowledge. Our approach features a hierarchical multi-modal article retrieval mechanism that moves beyond monolithic text entities. This retrieval considers article structure-aware features, including weighted textual components (e.g., headlines, body sections) and visual placement patterns, alongside multi-faceted similarity computations (content--visual, visual--visual, and discourse positioning). A subsequent contextual relevance refinement stage further enhances the retrieved information. The retrieved articles then serve as the knowledge base for caption generation: first, a VLM generates a concise image description; second, we segment relevant information from the retrieved articles based on this description; and finally, an LLM utilizes both the description and extracted knowledge to generate a comprehensive, contextually detailed caption. We participated in the ACM Multimedia EVENTA 2025 Challenge and achieved 5th place with an overall score of 0.2824 on the private test set of the OpenEvent-V1 dataset. Source code is publicly released at https://github.com/mf0212/EVENTA-Challange.

AI.tldr: A retrieval-augmented captioning framework that uses hierarchical article retrieval (structure-aware, multi-modal, multi-faceted similarity) and a two-stage VLM+LLM pipeline to generate context-rich image captions, achieving 5th place in ACM Multimedia EVENTA 2025 Challenge.
AI.motivation: Traditional image captioning methods fail to generate comprehensive context-rich descriptions for details not directly observable from visual cues (e.g., object attributes, event context, underlying significance).
AI.method: (1) Hierarchical multi-modal article retrieval using structure-aware features (headlines, body sections, visual placement patterns) and multi-faceted similarity (content-visual, visual-visual, discourse positioning). (2) Contextual relevance refinement. (3) Two-stage caption generation: VLM produces concise description → segment relevant article info → LLM fuses description + knowledge for final caption.
AI.result: 5th place in ACM Multimedia EVENTA 2025 Challenge with overall score 0.2824 on private test set of OpenEvent-V1 dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed retrieval-augmented approach effectively generates more contextually detailed captions by incorporating external knowledge from articles. Source code is publicly available.

title: Forged Calamity: Benchmark for Cross-Domain Synthetic Disaster Detection in the Age of Diffusion
summary: The rapid advancement of text-to-image diffusion models has enabled the creation of highly photorealistic synthetic images that closely resemble real photographs, making it increasingly difficult to distinguish authentic content from AI-generated fabrications. This poses challenges for cybersecurity, digital forensics, and disaster response, where fake imagery of floods, fires, or earthquakes can spread misinformation or disrupt emergency operations. To address this, we introduce Forged Calamity, a benchmark dataset for synthetic disaster detection containing 30,000 images, including 6,000 real and 24,000 synthetic samples generated by four diffusion models. Comprehensive experiments across fine-tuned and zero-shot settings reveal consistent weaknesses in current forensic approaches. Fine-tuned detectors perform well in-distribution but lose up to 50\% accuracy on unseen generators or disaster types, showing overfitting to model-specific artifacts. Zero-shot generalized detectors also struggle to maintain stable accuracy, with only limited resilience in a few representation-robust models. These findings highlight persistent generalization gaps and the urgent need for domain- and model-agnostic detection methods to ensure visual authenticity in the diffusion era.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "Forged Calamity," a 30,000-image benchmark (6,000 real, 24,000 synthetic from four diffusion models) for detecting AI-generated disaster imagery. Experiments show current detectors (fine-tuned and zero-shot) suffer severe generalization failures, losing up to 50% accuracy on unseen generators or disaster types, highlighting the urgent need for domain-agnostic forensic methods.
AI.motivation: The rapid advancement of text-to-image diffusion models makes synthetic images nearly indistinguishable from real photos, posing serious threats to cybersecurity, digital forensics, and disaster response (e.g., fake floods, fires, earthquakes spreading misinformation or disrupting emergency operations).
AI.method: Constructed Forged Calamity benchmark with 30,000 images (6,000 real, 24,000 synthetic from four diffusion models). Conducted comprehensive experiments in both fine-tuned and zero-shot settings to evaluate current forensic detection approaches.
AI.result: Fine-tuned detectors perform well in-distribution but lose up to 50% accuracy on unseen generators/disaster types due to overfitting. Zero-shot detectors also struggle to maintain stable accuracy, with only limited resilience in a few representation-robust models.
AI.conclusion: There are persistent generalization gaps in current synthetic image detectors, and there is an urgent need for domain- and model-agnostic detection methods to ensure visual authenticity in the diffusion era.

title: Rethinking Text-to-Image as Semantic-Aware Data Augmentation for Indoor Scene Recognition
summary: In the realm of computer vision, indoor image recognition presents challenges due to the intricate interplay of lighting conditions, occlusions, and diverse object arrangements within confined spaces. To address the lacks of training indoor images, we introduce a novel approach leveraging Stable Diffusion (SD) for the generation of synthetic images, which serve as a powerful data augmentation tool. The utilization of SD offers a principled framework for synthesizing diverse and realistic indoor scenes, thereby enriching the training data pool for robust indoor image recognition models. Experimental findings on the MIT Indoor Scene dataset reveal the potential of our proposed approach in enhancing the training of deep models when authentic data is limited. Furthermore, to prevent the misuse of SD synthetic images, we introduce a counter measure based on DIffusion Reconstruction Error (DIRE). The powerful DIRE presentation enables training robust classifiers only using lightweight deep models. Experiments show that our approach can perfectly recognize SD generated images with the accuracy of 100% using MobilenetV3.

AI.tldr: This paper uses Stable Diffusion to generate synthetic indoor images for data augmentation in scene recognition, and introduces DIRE (Diffusion Reconstruction Error) as a countermeasure to detect SD-generated images, achieving 100% detection accuracy with lightweight models like MobileNetV3.
AI.motivation: Indoor image recognition suffers from limited training data due to challenges like varying lighting, occlusions, and cluttered environments. The authors aim to address data scarcity by generating synthetic training images.
AI.method: The approach uses Stable Diffusion (SD) to synthesize realistic indoor scenes for data augmentation. Additionally, DIffusion Reconstruction Error (DIRE) is proposed as a detection method to distinguish SD-generated images from real ones, enabling robust classifier training with lightweight deep models (MobileNetV3).
AI.result: Experiments on the MIT Indoor Scene dataset show improved model performance when augmented with SD-generated images. The DIRE-based detector achieves 100% accuracy in recognizing SD-generated images using MobileNetV3.
AI.conclusion: Stable Diffusion effectively augments limited indoor scene datasets, and DIRE provides a perfect lightweight solution for detecting synthetic SD images, preventing potential misuse.

title: MolmoMotion: Forecasting Point Trajectories in 3D with Language Instruction
summary: Motion forecasting is central to visual intelligence: agents must anticipate how objects will move in order to plan actions, reason about physical interactions, and synthesize realistic futures. We argue that 3D points in world coordinates provide a general representation that is class-agnostic, view-stable, compact, and directly useful for downstream tasks. We formalize the task of goal-conditioned 3D point motion forecasting: given a short visual history, a set of 3D query points on an object of interest, and a language description of the intended goal, the model predicts the future 3D trajectory of each point. We introduce a full stack to study this task at scale: (1) MolmoMotion-1M is a large corpus of action-described, object-grounded 3D point trajectories annotated from 1.16M unconstrained videos; (2) PointMotionBench is a human-verified benchmark spanning 111 object categories and 61 motion types; and (3) MolmoMotion is a general motion forecasting model that supports both autoregressive coordinate prediction and flow-matching-based trajectory generation. MolmoMotion accurately predicts diverse motion patterns with different language instructions, and significantly outperforms existing motion prediction baselines on PointMotionBench. Finally, we show that the learned 3D motion prior transfers well to downstream applications: it improves training efficiency and generalization for robot manipulation, and its predicted trajectories provide effective motion guidance for generative models to synthesize videos with more realistic object motion.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework for 3D point motion forecasting from videos using language goal conditioning, including a large dataset (MolmoMotion-1M), a benchmark (PointMotionBench), and a model (MolmoMotion) that predicts future 3D trajectories of object points, outperforming baselines and transferring well to robotics and video generation.
AI.motivation: To address the need for a general, class-agnostic, view-stable, and compact motion forecasting representation using 3D world-coordinate points, enabling better downstream task performance in robotics and video generation.
AI.method: The approach includes: (1) constructing MolmoMotion-1M, a large-scale corpus of action-described 3D point trajectories from 1.16M videos; (2) creating PointMotionBench, a human-verified benchmark with 111 object categories and 61 motion types; and (3) developing MolmoMotion, a model supporting both autoregressive coordinate prediction and flow-matching-based trajectory generation conditioned on language goals.
AI.result: MolmoMotion accurately predicts diverse motion patterns with different language instructions and significantly outperforms existing motion prediction baselines on PointMotionBench. The learned 3D motion prior improves training efficiency and generalization for robot manipulation, and its predicted trajectories provide effective motion guidance for video generation.
AI.conclusion: 3D point motion forecasting with language goal conditioning is a powerful paradigm; the learned motion priors transfer effectively to downstream applications like robotics and video generation, suggesting broad utility for visual intelligence.

title: Experimental Analysis of Neural Network-Based Image Classification on the CIFAR-10 Dataset
summary: An experimental investigation of neural image classification on the CIFAR-10 benchmark is presented through fully connected and convolutional network formulations. The analysis emphasizes the complete learning pipeline: image vectorization, normalization, one-hot class encoding, supervised loss minimization, learning-rate selection, mini-batch training, convolutional feature extraction, max-pooling, and validation-based generalization assessment. A convolutional architecture with six convolutional layers and three max-pooling stages is evaluated for ten training epochs using a batch size of 128 and an Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001. The validation accuracy reaches approximately 74.77%, while the validation loss begins to increase after the middle of training despite continued reduction in training loss. The resulting behavior illustrates the practical difference between representation learning and memorization, and it provides a compact experimental baseline for future studies on regularization, data augmentation, deeper architectures, and reproducible image-classification education.

AI.tldr: This paper experimentally evaluates neural image classification on CIFAR-10 using both fully connected and convolutional networks. A 6-layer CNN with max-pooling achieves ~74.77% validation accuracy, but shows overfitting as validation loss increases mid-training while training loss continues decreasing.
AI.motivation: To provide a compact experimental baseline for image classification on CIFAR-10 that illustrates the practical difference between representation learning and memorization, serving as a reference for future studies on regularization, data augmentation, deeper architectures, and reproducible education.
AI.method: Complete learning pipeline including image vectorization, normalization, one-hot encoding, supervised loss minimization, learning-rate selection, mini-batch training, convolutional feature extraction, max-pooling, and validation-based generalization assessment. A CNN with 6 convolutional layers and 3 max-pooling stages trained for 10 epochs with batch size 128 and Adam optimizer (lr=0.001).
AI.result: Validation accuracy reaches ~74.77%. Validation loss increases after the middle of training despite continued reduction in training loss, indicating overfitting.
AI.conclusion: The observed behavior illustrates the distinction between genuine representation learning and memorization. The work provides a compact experimental baseline for future studies on regularization, data augmentation, deeper architectures, and reproducible image-classification education.

title: Multi-Modal Hyper-Graph Fusion for Low-Light Crowd Counting
summary: Crowd counting is a fundamental task in computer vision. However, crowd counting in low-light environments remains largely underexplored, despite its practical importance in the real world. Existing methods mainly focus on well-lit scenes or rely on single-modality Red-Green-Blue (RGB) representations, which often become unreliable under extreme darkness and complex non-uniform illumination. To handle this problem, we construct three new low-light crowd counting benchmarks, which consist of two synthetic datasets, SHA\_Dark and SHB\_Dark, and a real-world benchmark LC-Crowd (Low-light Crowd Dataset). Inspired by Retinex-based physical modeling, we introduce depth and Canny edge cues as complementary geometric and structural priors to enhance the intrinsic reflectance representation under low-light conditions. We propose a Multi-Modal Hyper-Graph Fusion module, which formulates RGB appearance, depth geometry, and edge structure cues as nodes in a unified hyper-graph and explicitly captures their high-order complementary relationships via dynamic hyperedge construction and message passing. Furthermore, to adaptively allocate computation in dense prediction, we propose a Deformable Rectangular Sparse Attention (DRSA) module, which concentrates computation on informative regions through anchor-aware estimation and adaptive rectangular window modeling. Based on these designs, we develop a unified Low-Light Counting Network (LCNet) for robust low-light crowd counting. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method achieves the best overall performance against existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods. The code is in the supplementary material. The datasets will be made public upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LCNet, a multi-modal network with hyper-graph fusion and deformable sparse attention for robust crowd counting in low-light environments, outperforming SOTA on three new benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Crowd counting in low-light environments is practically important but underexplored; existing methods fail under extreme darkness and non-uniform illumination as they rely on single-modality RGB representations.
AI.method: (1) Three new benchmarks (SHA_Dark, SHB_Dark, LC-Crowd); (2) Multi-Modal Hyper-Graph Fusion module combining RGB, depth, and edge cues via hyper-graph with dynamic hyperedge construction; (3) Deformable Rectangular Sparse Attention (DRSA) for adaptive computation allocation; (4) Unified Low-Light Counting Network (LCNet).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three benchmarks show the proposed method achieves the best overall performance against existing state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal fusion with high-order relational modeling and adaptive attention is effective for low-light crowd counting.

title: Technical Report for ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge: Leveraging DINOv3 for Robust Outdoor Scene Understanding in Field Robotics
summary: The GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge at the ICRA 2026 Workshop on Field Robotics evaluates dense semantic segmentation of off-road imagery over a fine-grained taxonomy of 64 classes and 11 evaluated non-void coarse categories. We present the first-place solution to this challenge. Our solution comprises two complementary improvements: (a) a network-level design that combines a self-supervised DINOv3 ViT-L/16 backbone, a ViT-Adapter, and a Mask2Former mask-classification decoder, together with a coarse-category auxiliary loss on the global [CLS] token; and (b) an inference-time aggregation strategy based on multi-scale and horizontal-flip test-time augmentation and an ensemble of the top three checkpoints selected using Codabench scores. Our method achieves an official composite score of 76.57%, consisting of 69.32% fine-class mIoU and 83.81% category-level mIoU, and ranks first on the final phase leaderboard: www.codabench.org/competitions/14257/#/results-tab.

AI.tldr: First-place solution for GOOSE 2D semantic segmentation challenge using DINOv3 ViT-L/16 backbone, ViT-Adapter, Mask2Former decoder, and multi-scale TTA ensemble, achieving 76.57% composite score.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of dense semantic segmentation of off-road imagery with a fine-grained taxonomy of 64 classes, enabling robust field robotics perception in unstructured environments.
AI.method: (a) Network: self-supervised DINOv3 ViT-L/16 backbone + ViT-Adapter + Mask2Former decoder with coarse-category auxiliary loss on [CLS] token; (b) Inference: multi-scale + horizontal-flip TTA, ensemble of top 3 checkpoints.
AI.result: Official composite score 76.57% (69.32% fine-class mIoU + 83.81% category-level mIoU), ranked 1st on final leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture with self-supervised pretraining, mask-classification decoding, and aggregation strategies yields state-of-the-art performance for fine-grained off-road semantic segmentation.

title: Aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition with patch-level self-supervised learning and expanded reciprocal re-ranking
summary: LiDAR place recognition determines one's position on a prior point cloud map. The most studied ground-level LiDAR place recognition suffers from pre-visit requirements, incomplete coverage, and limited perspectives. Using pre-acquired, full-coverage Airborne Laser Scanning (ALS) data as an aerial prior map overcomes these drawbacks, making cross-view place recognition necessary and advantageous. However, aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition faces significant challenges, including the domain gap between aerial and ground point clouds, and false positives during initial retrieval. To address these challenges, we present a novel retrieval and re-ranking framework for aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition. Based on the priors that neighboring point cloud patches share similar semantics with anchor patch, our retrieval network introduces patch-level self-supervised learning modules at multiple scales and integrates with scene-level learning to improve global feature discriminativeness between aerial and ground point clouds. Furthermore, leveraging the structured spatial distribution of ALS point clouds, we introduce an Expanded Reciprocal (ER) re-ranking algorithm to exploit neighborhood information maximally and refine each feature based on neighbor features, which are then used to update the similarity matrix for final ranking. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our retrieval network outperforms existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods, achieving a 9.8\% improvement in average Recall@1 and a 3.2\% improvement in average Recall@1\% on the CS-Urban-Scenes, while also showing the best performance on the CS-Campus3D dataset. Additionally, our ER re-ranking algorithm further boosts the average Recall@1 by 4.9\% on CS-Campus3D and 10.2\% on CS-Urban-Scenes without additional training.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a novel retrieval and re-ranking framework for aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition, using airborne laser scanning data as prior maps. It introduces multi-scale patch-level self-supervised learning alongside scene-level learning to bridge the domain gap between aerial and ground point clouds, plus an Expanded Reciprocal re-ranking algorithm to reduce false positives. Results show significant improvements over SOTA methods on two datasets.
AI.motivation: Ground-level LiDAR place recognition has limitations: it requires pre-visit, has incomplete coverage, and limited perspectives. Using pre-acquired full-coverage ALS data as aerial prior maps overcomes these drawbacks, making cross-view (aerial-ground) place recognition necessary and advantageous.
AI.method: 1) A retrieval network with patch-level self-supervised learning at multiple scales integrated with scene-level learning to improve cross-view discriminativeness. 2) An Expanded Reciprocal (ER) re-ranking algorithm that leverages structured spatial distribution of ALS data to exploit neighborhood information maximally and refine features.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA with 9.8% improvement in avg Recall@1 and 3.2% in avg Recall@1% on CS-Urban-Scenes. Best performance on CS-Campus3D. ER re-ranking further boosts avg Recall@1 by 4.9% on CS-Campus3D and 10.2% on CS-Urban-Scenes without additional training.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses domain gap and false positive challenges in aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets.

title: APT: Atomic Physical Transitions for Causal Video-Language Understanding
summary: Physical events are not understood by their names alone, but by the causal state changes that compose them. A clip-level label such as "bounce" can be correct while hiding the process that makes the event physically valid, from support loss and contact onset to rebound and settling. To make this hidden process explicit, we introduce Atomic Physical Transitions (APTs): minimal, temporally localized state changes that bind a visible cue to an active physical mechanism and before/after dynamical regimes. An APT chain represents a video as an ordered causal transition sequence rather than a single aggregate event label: event labels tell what happened; APT chains explain why it happened. To make APTs learnable by VLMs, we construct mixed-source APT data from human annotations and simulator ground truth, covering 14 transition types across contact, gravity, friction, and rotation/stability, with 27,303 timed instances over 1,246 trials. Using this data, we find that current VLMs miss transition-level physics, with zero-shot recall at most 14% and errors dominated by missed transitions. Direct fine-tuning on APT chains improves transition detection but causes event-level forgetting, indicating that the model learns a specialized answer format rather than a reusable physical representation. We therefore propose APT-Tune, a parameter-efficient recipe that teaches VLMs to use causal transitions without forgetting how to answer video questions. It combines image-pad-aware supervision, format-conditional co-training, and mechanism-conditioned domain-to-type decoding to make APT learning format-robust and physically grounded. With only 11 M LoRA parameters on Qwen3-VL-2B, APT-Tune substantially improves APT recall while also improving event-level video transfer. These results show that APTs are not a new answer format, but a human-aligned causal supervision signal for physical video understanding.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Atomic Physical Transitions (APTs)—minimal, temporally localized state changes that explain the causal mechanisms behind physical events (e.g., bounce, break). The authors build a dataset of 27,303 APT instances across 1,246 trials, show that current VLMs perform poorly (≤14% recall) on APT detection, and propose APT-Tune, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that improves APT recall without sacrificing event-level video understanding.
AI.motivation: Physical events (e.g., "bounce") are typically labeled at the clip level, which hides the underlying causal process (support loss, contact onset, rebound, settling). The paper aims to make this hidden causal structure explicit and learnable by VLMs.
AI.method:
- Define Atomic Physical Transitions (APTs): minimal, temporally localized state changes with visible cue, active mechanism, and before/after regimes.
- Build mixed-source APT dataset from human annotations and simulator ground truth, covering 14 transition types across contact, gravity, friction, and rotation/stability.
- Propose APT-Tune: a parameter-efficient fine-tuning recipe combining image-pad-aware supervision, format-conditional co-training, and mechanism-conditioned domain-to-type decoding.
AI.result:
- Zero-shot VLMs achieve at most 14% recall on APT detection; errors dominated by missed transitions.
- Direct fine-tuning improves APT detection but causes event-level forgetting.
- APT-Tune (only 11M LoRA parameters on Qwen3-VL-2B) substantially improves APT recall while also improving event-level video transfer.
AI.conclusion: APTs are not a new answer format but a human-aligned causal supervision signal for physical video understanding. Causal transition-level reasoning is crucial for robust physical video understanding.

title: Bridging Creative Intent and Visual Quality: Creator-Driven Recurrent Video Generation with Agentic Feedback Loops
comment: Accepted to the Workshop on Human-AI Co-Creativity at ICML 2026
summary: Generative AI has made content creation increasingly accessible, but many AI-generated videos lack narrative coherence and creative direction, issues that become more substantial at longer durations. Unlike coding, where AI generation benefits from reliable feedback and techniques such as recurrent self-improvement, video generation requires subjective feedback about plot, scenes, and narrative, which naturally motivates approaches that incorporate human creative direction. We introduce CHIEF, a human-AI co-creation video generation framework that places the creator at the center of human-in-the-loop iterative video refinement, and supports them by providing automatic subjective feedback. The creator incorporates their creative direction by driving each iteration, while their revisions are incorporated by a specialized refiner agent. The feedback loop is generated by persona-conditioned multimodal LLMs that watch generated videos and produce subjective critique from the audience perspectives, providing feedback that self-evaluation alone cannot capture. To test the effectiveness of our proposed framework, we work with high school and college students with no prior filmmaking experience to create videos, from short 1-minute videos to a complete short 10-minute film with a complicated plot.

AI.tldr: CHIEF is a human-AI co-creation framework for video generation that puts creators in an iterative loop with AI, using persona-conditioned LLMs to generate audience-style subjective feedback, enabling non-experts to produce coherent narrative videos from short clips to a complete 10-minute film.
AI.motivation: AI-generated videos lack narrative coherence and creative direction, especially at longer durations. Unlike coding, video generation requires subjective, human-driven feedback about plot and narrative, motivating a human-in-the-loop approach.
AI.method: CHIEF places the creator at the center of iterative video refinement; a refiner agent incorporates human revisions; persona-conditioned multimodal LLMs watch videos and produce subjective audience-style critique for feedback.
AI.result: Tested with high school and college students with no prior filmmaking experience; they created videos ranging from short 1-minute clips to a complete 10-minute short film with a complex plot.
AI.conclusion: Human-AI co-creation with persona-based subjective feedback can enable non-experts to produce narratively coherent videos, addressing a key limitation of standalone generative AI for video.

title: Hallucination Detection and Correction in Medical VLMs via Counter-Evidence Verification
summary: Vision-Language models (VLMs) reliability in medical diagnosis is challenged by trust-undermining hallucinations. Existing hallucination detection approaches mainly focus on identifying factual inconsistencies between generated text and reference data. While some studies analyze where models attend in images, they seldom verify whether such attention truly reflects the visual evidence supporting the generated text. To address this gap, we propose Co}unter-Evidence Verification (CoEV), a training-free plug-and-play framework that detects and corrects hallucinations through evidence-based factual consistency verification. CoEV performs bidirectional verification between textual assertions and visual evidence, testing whether each statement is supported by its corresponding evidence region, and assigns each statement into a four-quadrant diagnostic map capturing combinations of text factuality and visual grounding. CoEV detects hallucinated content and serves as a post hoc refinement tool, correcting hallucinations without retraining. Extensive experiments on four medical datasets show that CoEV combats hallucinations in VLMs.For hallucination detection, CoEV consistently outperforms existing methods, improving average PR-AUC and ROC-AUC by 3.0% and 3.9% absolute points respectively, with notable gains of up to 18.5% in specific VQA scenarios. For hallucination correction, it improves Micro-F1 by up to 12.5%, reduces hallucination rates by over 11.9% on medical report generation, and also boosts medical VQA accuracy. These results show that CoEV enables reliable detection and correction of hallucinations, providing clinicians with dependable, evidence-based cues for diagnosis. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: CoEV is a training-free, plug-and-play framework that detects and corrects hallucinations in Vision-Language Models for medical diagnosis by performing bidirectional verification between textual assertions and visual evidence, outperforming existing methods across multiple medical datasets.
AI.motivation: VLMs in medical diagnosis suffer from trust-undermining hallucinations, and existing detection methods focus on factual inconsistencies without verifying whether model attention truly reflects visual evidence supporting generated text.
AI.method: Proposes CoEV (Counter-Evidence Verification), a training-free plug-and-play framework that performs bidirectional verification between textual assertions and visual evidence, assigning each statement to a four-quadrant diagnostic map capturing combinations of text factuality and visual grounding.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods in hallucination detection (average PR-AUC +3.0%, ROC-AUC +3.9%, up to 18.5% in specific VQA scenarios). For correction, improves Micro-F1 by up to 12.5%, reduces hallucination rates by over 11.9% on medical report generation, and boosts medical VQA accuracy.
AI.conclusion: CoEV enables reliable detection and correction of hallucinations, providing clinicians with dependable, evidence-based cues for diagnosis.

title: Intrinsic 4D Gaussian Segmentation from Scene Cues
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 7 tables. Includes supplementary material. Preprint
summary: Dynamic 4D Gaussian Splatting reconstructs deforming scenes with high fidelity and is increasingly adopted as a representation for dynamic 3D scenes. Putting such a scene to use, for editing, manipulation or motion analysis, first requires segmenting it: grouping the Gaussian primitives into coherent objects. Current pipelines obtain this grouping by importing 2D masks from foundation models such as SAM and lifting or distilling them into the Gaussian representation. In dynamic scenes these masks must be generated across many frames and views, which is costly, and the resulting segmentation can depend strongly on the quality and consistency of those external masks. We ask how much object-level structure can instead be recovered from the Gaussians themselves, and propose Intrinsic-GS, a training-free, mask-free method that builds a sparse affinity graph over Gaussian primitives from appearance, orientation, scale, deformation-trajectory and non-learned rendered-boundary cues. The graph is partitioned with Leiden community detection, requiring no foundation model and no learned feature field. On the standard 4D Gaussian segmentation benchmarks, Neu3D and HyperNeRF, Intrinsic-GS recovers substantial object structure without mask supervision, reaching 0.746 mIoU on Neu3D and 0.575 on HyperNeRF; on Neu3D, a geometry-only variant reaches 0.902 mIoU, matching SAM-supervised TRASE. On HyperNeRF, Intrinsic-GS runs 12.5x faster than the mask-generation and feature-rendering stages used by mask-supervised pipelines. These results suggest that much of the segmentation signal is already encoded in the Gaussians themselves, offering a fast, mask-free direction for 3D and 4D Gaussian segmentation that may also point toward more generalizable, robust segmentation in settings where external masks are unreliable or expensive.

AI.tldr: Intrinsic-GS is a training-free, mask-free method that segments dynamic 4D Gaussian scenes by building a sparse affinity graph from intrinsic Gaussian cues (appearance, geometry, deformation) and applying Leiden community detection, achieving competitive accuracy (0.746 mIoU on Neu3D, 0.575 on HyperNeRF) while running 12.5x faster than mask-supervised pipelines.
AI.motivation: Current pipelines for segmenting dynamic 4D Gaussian scenes rely on importing 2D masks from foundation models like SAM across many frames and views, which is costly, and the segmentation quality heavily depends on the consistency of those external masks. The authors ask how much object-level structure can instead be recovered from the Gaussians themselves without external supervision.
AI.method: Intrinsic-GS builds a sparse affinity graph over Gaussian primitives using cues from appearance, orientation, scale, deformation-trajectory, and non-learned rendered-boundary features. The graph is then partitioned using Leiden community detection—no foundation model, no learned feature field, and no mask supervision required.
AI.result: Intrinsic-GS achieves 0.746 mIoU on Neu3D and 0.575 mIoU on HyperNeRF without mask supervision. A geometry-only variant reaches 0.902 mIoU on Neu3D, matching SAM-supervised TRASE. On HyperNeRF, it runs 12.5x faster than mask-generation and feature-rendering stages of supervised pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Much of the segmentation signal is already encoded intrinsically in the Gaussians themselves. Intrinsic-GS offers a fast, mask-free direction for 3D/4D Gaussian segmentation that may generalize better to settings where external masks are unreliable or expensive.

title: Spiking Pyramid Wavelet Transformation for High-efficient and Low-energy Image Restoration
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) have garnered significant interest in computer vision due to their potential for efficiency and biological inspiration. While spiking CNN-based methods have shown promise for image restoration (IR) tasks, their performance is constrained by the inherent receptive field limitations of CNN operations. In the paper, we explore the benefits of discrete wavelet transformation and propose a spiking pyramid wavelet-based model (SPWM) for high-efficient and low-energy target. Specifically, we develop a spiking dual pyramid wavelet (SDPW) block to model long-range dependency and exploit the properties of the degradation in the wavelet domain. Experimental results on several benchmarks demonstrate that SPWM significantly lowers computational costs and energy consumption while maintaining image quality. Our method showcases the potential of SNNs in the field of IR, offering new insights for future applications of resource-limited devices.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Spiking Pyramid Wavelet-based Model (SPWM) for image restoration that uses discrete wavelet transformation and spiking dual pyramid wavelet blocks to achieve long-range dependency modeling while significantly reducing computational costs and energy consumption.
AI.motivation: SNNs show promise for image restoration tasks, but spiking CNN-based methods are limited by small receptive fields of CNN operations, restricting their performance.
AI.method: The authors propose SPWM, incorporating a Spiking Dual Pyramid Wavelet (SDPW) block that leverages discrete wavelet transformation to model long-range dependencies and exploit degradation properties in the wavelet domain.
AI.result: SPWM significantly lowers computational costs and energy consumption while maintaining competitive image quality on several benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates SNNs' potential in image restoration, providing new insights for resource-limited device applications.

title: On-Manifold Variational Learning with Heat-Kernel Priors
summary: Learning unsupervised representations of medical imaging cohorts can reveal clinically meaningful prototypes without expert labels, which are often noisy and fail to capture true pathological heterogeneity. However, existing deep latent-variable models estimate Gaussian mixture priors via Euclidean averaging, producing prototypes that drift off the curved data manifold and degenerate as the number of sub-populations grows. We propose a manifold-anchored variational framework built on a geometry-aware Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, whose M-step selects each sub-population prototype as the graph medoid with the highest diffusion centrality on a heat-kernel-weighted latent graph, ensuring that every prototype remains on-manifold. A Dirichlet energy regularizer enforces geometric smoothness of the latent space, and a per-sub-population uncertainty score enables label-free quality assessment. \rev{The manifold-anchored EM is a general-purpose geometric tool that extends standard EM and applies readily to other latent-variable models beyond this setting.} On cardiac scar and brain MRI benchmarks, our framework attains the highest accuracy among all compared methods, produces the sharpest prototypes reported to date, and remains stable at large sub-population counts where all baselines degenerate.

AI.tldr: Proposes a manifold-anchored variational framework using geometry-aware EM with graph medoids and diffusion centrality to learn on-manifold prototypes for unsupervised medical imaging analysis, outperforming baselines on cardiac and brain MRI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing deep latent-variable models estimate Gaussian mixture priors via Euclidean averaging, causing prototypes to drift off the curved data manifold and degenerate as sub-populations grow.
AI.method: Proposes manifold-anchored EM where M-step selects prototypes as graph medoids via diffusion centrality on heat-kernel-weighted latent graph, plus Dirichlet energy regularizer for geometric smoothness and per-sub-population uncertainty scores.
AI.result: Achieves highest accuracy among all compared methods, produces sharpest prototypes, and remains stable at large sub-population counts where baselines degenerate on cardiac scar and brain MRI benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The manifold-anchored EM is a general-purpose geometric tool extending standard EM applicable to other latent-variable models.

title: LandslideAgent with Multimodal LandslideBench: A Domain-Rule-Augmented Agent for Autonomous Landslide Identification and Analysis
summary: Intelligent landslide hazard interpretation is critical for disaster prevention, yet current paradigms struggle to simultaneously extract visual features and high-level geoscientific semantics, while general-purpose vision-language models (VLMs) suffer from perceptual limitations and domain hallucinations in complex geological scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose an instruction-driven agentic framework comprising three components. First, LandslideBench, a multimodal fine-grained dataset with seven subtype labels, high-resolution imagery, pixel-level masks, and high-quality textual descriptions, is constructed via multi-VLM cross-validation and interactive annotation. Then, LandslideVLM, a landslide-oriented VLM, is fine-tuned via LoRA on LandslideBench to enhance geological semantic understanding. Finally, LandslideAgent, a domain rule-enhanced agent taking LandslideVLM as its cognitive backbone, employs a dual-rule controller incorporating structured report metadata constraints and cross-validation identification constraints to regulate automated tool invocation. Experiments demonstrate that LandslideBench provides effective baselines across five mainstream models on fine-grained classification and semantic segmentation. LandslideVLM achieves accuracy improvements of 10.96%, 32.87%, and 15.91% on landslide discrimination, fine-grained classification, and semantic description quality, respectively. LandslideAgent further enables autonomous multi-source spatial data inference, realizing full-process intelligence for landslide identification and analysis.

AI.tldr: A framework integrating a landslide benchmark dataset (LandslideBench), a domain-specific VLM (LandslideVLM), and a rule-enhanced agent (LandslideAgent) to enable intelligent, full-process landslide hazard interpretation from visual and geoscientific data.
AI.motivation: Existing models struggle to combine visual features with high-level geoscientific semantics for landslide interpretation, and general VLMs suffer from domain hallucinations in complex geological scenarios.
AI.method: Three components: (1) LandslideBench - multimodal dataset via multi-VLM cross-validation and annotation; (2) LandslideVLM - LoRA-fine-tuned VLM on LandslideBench; (3) LandslideAgent - domain rule-enhanced agent with dual-rule controller for automated tool invocation.
AI.result: LandslideVLM improves accuracy by 10.96% (discrimination), 32.87% (fine-grained classification), and 15.91% (semantic description). LandslideAgent enables autonomous multi-source spatial data inference.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework achieves full-process intelligence for landslide identification and analysis, effectively addressing perceptual and hallucination issues in complex geological settings.

title: BrainFusionNet: a deep learning and XAI model to understand local, global, and sequential features of MRI images for improved brain tumour detection
summary: The noise of Magnetic Resonance Imaging MRI poses challenges for Deep Learning DL when tumor boundaries are obscured tumor location and appearance are complex Therefore we develop BrainFusionNet that combines Convolutional Neural Networks CNNs Vision Transformers ViT and Gated Recurrent Units GRUs to extract spatial contextual and sequential features from MRI images for improved brain tumor classification Furthermore explainable AI such as SHAP LIME and GradCAM are integrated to visualise and highlight image regions that contribute to BrainFusionNets decisionmaking process The proposed BrainFusionNet model is evaluated on two publicly available MRI datasets Kfold validation suggests 98 accuracy on both datasets The model was compared with the six stateoftheart SOTA CNNs and transfer learning Among the SOTA CNNs DenseNet121 and VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy of 96 The novelty of BrainFusionNet is that the hybrid model effectively extracts local and global features from MRI images even in smallscale tumor regions and small tumor sizes The model has a balanced sequential CNN architecture to capture lowlevel and deeperlayer features a customized ViT that captures local features stabilizes gradient flow and reduces the risk of vanishing gradients during MRI image training The CNN and ViT outputs are fed into a GRU for final classification Furthermore we analyze pixel intensities to determine whether MRI image quality affects image classification Our findings are very novel in image interpretation as we found that the distribution of pixel intensities in MRI images affects DL performance

AI.tldr: BrainFusionNet is a hybrid deep learning model combining CNNs, Vision Transformers, and GRUs for brain tumor classification from MRI images, achieving 98% accuracy on two public datasets, with explainable AI tools (SHAP, LIME, GradCAM) integrated for interpretability.
AI.motivation: MRI noise makes tumor boundaries unclear, and tumor location/appearance vary widely, challenging standard deep learning approaches for accurate brain tumor classification.
AI.method: The model integrates CNNs (for low-level features), Vision Transformers (for local feature capture and gradient stabilization), and GRUs (for sequential reasoning), with SHAP/LIME/GradCAM for explainability. Pixel intensity analysis is also performed to study image quality effects.
AI.result: Achieves 98% accuracy on two public MRI datasets using K-fold validation, outperforming SOTA CNNs like DenseNet121 and VGG16 (96% accuracy).
AI.conclusion: The hybrid architecture effectively extracts both local and global features from MRI data, even for small tumors, and pixel intensity distributions influence DL performance—a novel finding in image interpretation.

title: Moving Beyond Diversity: Visual Token Pruning as Subspace Reconstruction for Efficient VLMs
summary: Despite their remarkable performance, Vision Language Models (VLMs) incur substantial computational overhead due to the large number of visual tokens. While diversity maximization has become a dominant strategy for token reduction, existing methods rely on cosine-based normalized similarity that discards magnitude information, failing to faithfully approximate the original feature representation and leading to suboptimal performance, particularly on compositional multi-skill reasoning tasks. In this paper, we introduce SPARE, a subspace reconstruction method that reformulates token pruning as a column subset selection problem and explicitly minimizes reconstruction error. By iteratively selecting tokens with large projection residuals, SPARE performs reconstruction-driven pruning beyond angular diversity. Moreover, we reveal a counterintuitive anti-relevance phenomenon: tokens with lower image-text relevance score can better preserve contextual information. Based on this finding, we incorporate anti-relevance into SPARE as an additional selection criterion to promote context-aware token selection. Extensive experiments across multiple VLMs and benchmarks demonstrate that SPARE consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, with strong gains on compositional tasks. When applied to LLaVA, SPARE removes up to 94% of visual tokens while retaining 95% of the baseline performance, all in a fully training-free manner.

AI.tldr: SPARE is a training-free token pruning method for VLMs that reformulates token reduction as a subspace reconstruction problem, selecting tokens by minimizing reconstruction error and leveraging an anti-relevance phenomenon where less relevant tokens better preserve context, achieving up to 94% token reduction with 95% performance retention.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM token reduction methods rely on cosine-based similarity that discards magnitude information, leading to poor approximations of original features and suboptimal performance, especially on compositional multi-skill reasoning tasks.
AI.method: SPARE formulates token pruning as a column subset selection problem, iteratively selecting tokens with large projection residuals to minimize reconstruction error. It also incorporates an anti-relevance criterion based on the finding that lower image-text relevance tokens better preserve contextual information.
AI.result: SPARE achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple VLMs and benchmarks, with strong gains on compositional tasks. On LLaVA, it removes up to 94% of visual tokens while retaining 95% of baseline performance, fully training-free.
AI.conclusion: Reconstruction-driven pruning with anti-relevance selection offers a more faithful and effective token reduction strategy than cosine-based diversity maximization for VLMs.

title: Multi-Class Brain Tumor Classification Using Advanced Deep Learning Models: A Comparative Study
summary: Despite recent advancements in deep learning, accurately classifying brain tumors from MRI images continues to pose challenges. In this research, we present a comprehensive evaluation of five different convolutional neural networks (CNN) architectures, including a customized baseline model and four pre-trained models - for use in classifying multi-class brain tumors using a clinically-sourced dataset of approximately 10,000 MRI images. We have utilized five different architectures; VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet121, and EfficientNetB0, which were all tested and trained within an identical experimental framework. Performance was measured by both overall accuracy and tumor-wise recall as a means to measure the clinically-relevant performance of each architecture. We found that EfficientNetB0 had the best overall classification accuracy at 95%, when compared to the other architectures tested; specifically VGG16 (94.37%), VGG19 (92.29%), DenseNet121 (90.91%) and the customized CNN (78.00%). An especially important finding of our research was the considerable improvement in detecting meningiomas; specifically, while simple CNNs could detect meningiomas with a recall rate of approximately 20%, EfficientNetB0 was able to detect meningiomas with a recall rate of 89%. Meningiomas are often difficult to detect because they can appear very subtly on MRI images. Additionally, an interesting finding was that the deeper VGG19 performed worse than the shallower VGG16. This indicates that in many cases the architectural efficiency of a CNN model may be more important than its depth when working with medical images. Overall, EfficientNetB0 appears to provide the optimal trade-off between classification accuracy, number of parameters used in the model and clinically meaningful performance.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates five CNN architectures (custom CNN, VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0) for brain tumor classification from ~10,000 MRI images. EfficientNetB0 achieved the highest accuracy (95%) and significantly improved meningioma detection (89% recall vs. ~20% for simple CNNs).
AI.motivation: Accurately classifying brain tumors from MRI images remains challenging despite deep learning advances; meningiomas are especially difficult to detect due to subtle appearance on MRI.
